title: After Arriving in the Netherlands: What to Do First
metaTitle: After Arriving in the Netherlands | BSN, Registration, DigiD, Insurance, Banking
subtitle: Your practical first-step guide after landing: municipality registration, BSN, residence permit collection, DigiD, health insurance, banking, mobile setup, and first-week admin.
description: A practical guide to what to do after arriving in the Netherlands, including municipality registration, BSN, residence permit collection, DigiD, health insurance, banking, and first-week setup.

image.alt: A man with glasses and a denim shirt sits at a wooden desk overlooking a Dutch canal, focused on his laptop displaying a 'First Week in the Netherlands' checklist and writing in a notebook, surrounded by a passport, a smartphone, and books like 'Relocation Tasks'.

heroCta.supportingText: Use the First 90 Days Planner and Moving Checklist to map registration, BSN, banking, insurance, and housing setup.

quickAnswers.label: MUNICIPALITY REGISTRATION
quickAnswers.value: Usually within 5 days of arrival if staying more than 4 months.

quickAnswers.label: BSN
quickAnswers.value: Needed for government admin, DigiD, and many practical setups.

quickAnswers.label: HEALTH INSURANCE
quickAnswers.value: If Dutch basic insurance is required, it usually must be arranged within 4 months.

quickAnswers.label: RESIDENCE DOCUMENT
quickAnswers.value: Collect from the IND location named in your message or letter if applicable.

sections.heading: What this page helps with
body: Arriving in the Netherlands is only the start. Most expats still need to complete registration, permit collection, insurance, banking, and digital government setup. The exact order can differ depending on your route, but municipality registration, BSN-linked admin, and residence-document handling are among the biggest first priorities.

This page focuses on practical first-week and early post-arrival actions—not the full visa application process. Use it as your main post-arrival hub and confirm any obligations with official sources, since your situation may differ.

sections.heading: Your first priorities after arrival
body: A typical sequence many expats follow is summarised below. Your route (e.g. highly skilled migrant, student, partner, EU citizen) can change timing or requirements, so treat this as a guide, not a fixed rule.
steps: Collect your residence permit if the IND has notified you
Set up phone and internet basics
Tackle first-week and first-month admin (GP, transport, housing follow-up)

ctaBlock.title: Map your first 90 days
ctaBlock.supportingText: Use the First 90 Days Planner to order your registration, insurance, banking, and housing setup after arrival.

sections.heading: Register with your municipality
body: If you are staying in the Netherlands for more than 4 months, you generally must register with the municipality where you live. According to Government.nl, this should be done within 5 days of arrival. Registration leads to entry in the BRP (Personal Records Database) and to BSN handling.
In practice you usually need an appointment. Bring route-relevant identity and address documents; the exact list can vary by municipality and situation (e.g. EU vs non-EU, employed vs student).

callout.title: Official source
callout.text: Government.nl – What to arrange when moving to the Netherlands: registration within 5 days if staying more than 4 months.
callout.linkLabel: Government.nl – What to arrange when moving to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Your BSN: one of the most important arrival steps
body: The BSN (Burgerservicenummer) is your citizen service number. You need it for dealings with Dutch authorities, and it is used for health insurance and DigiD-related admin. Netherlands Worldwide explains what a BSN is and where you can later find it.
bullets: Municipality registration — your BSN is typically issued or confirmed at registration
Health insurance — insurers and care providers use it
Tax and work admin — employer and tax authority use it
Salary and payroll — in many cases your employer needs it

callout.title: Official sources
callout.text: Netherlands Worldwide: What is a BSN? Where can I find my BSN?

sections.heading: Collect your residence permit if applicable
body: If the IND has notified you that your document is ready, collection is by appointment. You must collect it at the location stated by the IND. Sometimes original documents are returned during the same collection appointment.
bullets: Wait for the IND message or letter
Bring passport, appointment code, and prior permit if relevant

sections.heading: Set up DigiD and online government access
body: With a BSN, many people can apply for DigiD. MijnOverheid is the government portal used for personal government communication and access to records. This becomes useful early for admin, letters, and later for tax, benefits, and official communication.

links.label: DigiD for expats in the Netherlands

sections.heading: Check whether you need Dutch health insurance
body: In many cases, people living and working in the Netherlands need Dutch basic health insurance. Netherlands Worldwide states that if you are required to have it, you generally must arrange it within 4 months of arrival; there can be fines if you do not do so on time. There are exceptions, so confirm your insurance position if unsure.
Do not assume everyone needs it immediately—people with income or coverage outside the Netherlands may need to verify their situation. Compare insurers before choosing.

services.name: Independer
services.description: Insurance comparison site often used to compare Dutch health insurance options.

links.label: Health insurance in the Netherlands for expats

sections.heading: Open a Dutch bank account
body: Many arrivals want a Dutch account quickly for salary, rent, subscriptions, and daily spending. Banks often ask for identity, BSN, and address-related information; onboarding can differ by provider.

links.label: Open a bank account in the Netherlands

sections.heading: Get your phone, connectivity, and basic setup sorted
body: SIM and mobile setup is one of the easier first-week tasks. It is often needed quickly for banking, housing, delivery, appointments, and everyday admin.

sections.heading: Stabilise your address and living setup
body: Temporary housing is often part of the first days or weeks. Long-term setup may include rent deposit, furniture basics, utilities, and local admin. Housing decisions affect registration timing and practical settlement.

sections.heading: What may differ by route or household
body: Workers, students, partner or family movers, and self-employed people often have different first priorities and extra admin. The table below summarises typical focus areas; your situation may differ.

rows: Employer may require BSN and bank details quickly
Check if student insurance or basic package applies
Municipality registration for all, school or childcare
Each family member may need registration and documents
School or childcare sign-up, GP, routines
Registration and BSN for children; school placement timing
Banking, KVK and admin follow-up, insurance position
Business registration and tax registration after arrival

sections.heading: Common first-week and first-month costs after arrival
body: The figures below are indicative planning ranges. Costs vary by city, provider, housing, and personal situation. Amsterdam, Utrecht, Rotterdam, and The Hague often sit at the higher end for accommodation and some services.

rows: Some documents or services may have a fee
Depends on city, type, and length
Varies by bank; international transfer fees may apply

links.label: Cost of moving to the Netherlands

body: Use these official sources to confirm rules and procedures. Obligations can differ by visa, nationality, and personal situation.

exampleScenarios.title: Highly skilled migrant arriving for work
exampleScenarios.summary: First priorities: municipality registration, BSN, bank account for salary, health insurance. Likely bottlenecks: appointment lead time, proof of address. Use the First 90 Days Planner and Document Readiness Checker.

exampleScenarios.title: Student arriving before studies begin
exampleScenarios.summary: First priorities: registration, BSN, housing stability, health insurance (student or basic). Likely bottlenecks: housing that allows registration, insurance deadline. Use the Arrival Planner and Moving Checklist.

exampleScenarios.title: Couple or family arriving together
exampleScenarios.summary: First priorities: municipality registration for all, BSNs, bank account, health insurance, school or childcare if applicable. Likely bottlenecks: documents for each person, appointment availability. Use the First 90 Days Planner with household options.

exampleScenarios.title: Self-employed mover settling in
exampleScenarios.summary: First priorities: registration, BSN, bank account, KVK and tax follow-up, health insurance. Likely bottlenecks: business registration timing, proof of address. Use the Relocation Cost Estimator and First 90 Days Planner.

scenariosSectionIntro: Different situations lead to different first priorities and bottlenecks. Use the tools below to prefill a plan that fits your scenario.

servicesIntro: These services are commonly used by expats for banking, insurance, housing, and mobile setup. Compare options and confirm terms on providers’ sites.

faq.q: What should I do first after arriving in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Most expats prioritise municipality registration (within 5 days if staying more than 4 months), then BSN, then residence permit collection if the IND has notified you. After that, DigiD, bank account, health insurance, and mobile setup are typical next steps. Your route may differ—use the First 90 Days Planner to order your tasks.

faq.q: How soon do I need to register with the municipality?
faq.a: Government.nl states that if you are staying more than 4 months, you should register with the municipality where you live within 5 days of arrival. In practice you usually need an appointment, so book as soon as you know your address.

faq.q: When do I get a BSN?
faq.a: You typically receive or confirm your BSN when you register with the municipality. In many cases it is issued at the registration appointment or sent shortly after. Your municipality can confirm the exact process.

faq.q: Do I need DigiD straight away?
faq.a: You need a BSN before you can apply for DigiD, and often a Dutch bank account or other verification. So DigiD usually comes after registration and banking. It is useful early for government letters and admin—start the process as soon as you have your BSN.

faq.q: When do I need Dutch health insurance?
faq.a: If you are required to have Dutch basic health insurance (e.g. because you work or live here), Netherlands Worldwide states it usually must be arranged within 4 months of arrival. There are exceptions; confirm your position with an insurer or official source.

faq.q: How do I collect my residence permit?
faq.a: When the IND notifies you that your document is ready, you must book a collection appointment and collect it at the location they specify. Bring your passport and any documents they ask for. See IND’s page on appointment to collect document and IND desks and locations.

faq.q: Can I open a bank account right away?
faq.a: Many banks require a BSN and proof of address, so in practice people often open an account in the first or second week after registration. Some providers offer expat-friendly onboarding; requirements differ. See our guide to opening a bank account in the Netherlands.

faq.q: What should students do after arriving?
faq.a: Students typically need to register with the municipality, get a BSN, arrange health insurance (student or basic package depending on situation), and secure housing that allows registration. Enrolment and any visa or permit conditions also apply. Use the Arrival Planner and First 90 Days Planner to order your steps.

faq.q: What should families do after arriving?
faq.a: Each family member usually needs to be registered and may receive a BSN. Families often prioritise a bank account, health insurance, and school or childcare registration. Document requirements and appointment lead times can be heavier—use the First 90 Days Planner with household options.

faq.q: Which tools should I use next?
faq.a: Start with the First 90 Days Planner to map registration, BSN, banking, insurance, and housing. Use the Moving Checklist for a full pre- and post-arrival list, the Document Readiness Checker to confirm your documents, and the Relocation Cost Estimator to plan costs.
